5 Ways to Pull Values Out of a JavaScript Object
JavaScript

Objects are one of the data types you touch the most in JavaScript, so it's worth knowing all the ways to grab values out of them. This post walks through five: dot notation, bracket notation, destructuring, optional chaining, and the Object.keys/values/entries trio. A quick reference you can come back to.

Why You Shouldn't Call hasOwnProperty Directly (and What ESLint Wants Instead)
hasOwnProperty
Object.prototype
Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty

ESLint has a rule that yells at you for calling hasOwnProperty directly on an object. At first it feels overly strict, but there's a real reason behind it. This post walks through why obj.hasOwnProperty('key') can break, how an object property can shadow the prototype method, and why Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call is the safer way to go.

JavaScript Core Concepts (31) - Objects - Arrays

In JavaScript, an array is actually a kind of object. This post walks through how arrays work — reading values, pushing data, and a few weird quirks like adding properties to an array or skipping indexes and ending up with empty slots. If you've ever wondered why your array length didn't change after setting a property, this one's for you.

JavaScript Core Concepts (30): Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy for Objects

Objects in JavaScript are passed by reference, which means copying them is trickier than it looks. In this post we'll dig into shallow copy vs deep copy — how Object.assign and the spread syntax both fall short, why the JSON.stringify trick works but has nasty edge cases, and what to reach for when you need a real deep clone.

This chapter is the exercise for object references. The whole point is to make sure the "objects are passed by reference" idea actually stuck. We walk through a tricky little snippet step by step, tracing memory pointers and drawing out exactly why a and b end up the way they do.

JavaScript Core Concepts (27) — Objects: How Object References Actually Work

Last time we talked about pass-by-reference for objects, but the explanation was still pretty fuzzy. So this time let's actually see how it works under the hood — what happens in memory when you copy a primitive, and what happens when you assign an object to another variable and change its properties.

JavaScript Core Concepts (26): Objects — Why Changing One Object Changes Another

Time to talk about passing objects by reference. If you don't get how reference semantics work in JavaScript, it's really easy to step on a landmine. We'll walk through call by value vs call by reference, why changing one variable can secretly change another, and the simplest way to spot when reference behavior is in play.

JavaScript Core (25): Why Reading a Missing Object Property Gives You undefined (and Sometimes Crashes)

Reading a property that doesn't exist on an object hands you undefined — but go one level deeper and suddenly JavaScript throws an error instead. This post digs into why that happens, how operator precedence and associativity cause it, why primitives can't hold their own properties, and the two simple ways to avoid the crash.
